notes.note: The salutation is corrupt in all manuscript traditions: the obelus marks the name as unreadable. The proquaestor addressed is identified from context as Cn. Sallustius, who served under Cicero in Cilicia; the transmitted "CANINI" is a scribal garble of some form of his name or title.

The lex Iulia de repetundis of 59 BC (Caesar's consulship), which among other provisions regulated the submission of provincial accounts; Bibulus was apparently allowing his staff to skip the formal accounting, while Cicero insists on strict compliance.

Apamea on the Orontes (modern Afamia, Syria) was a key garrison town on the border between Cicero's Cilician command and Bibulus's Syrian province; Cicero had stationed troops there against the Parthian threat and withdrew them when the alarm subsided.

notes.anchor: At Laodicea I expect to take sureties
notes.note: Laodicea ad Lycum, the administrative centre of the Cibyratic conventus in Cicero's province; Cicero planned to deposit his financial sureties there before departing, avoiding the risk of transporting public monies by sea.

Bibulus wrote to Cicero solely to ask a favour regarding his son's admission to the college of augurs, a prestigious priestly office; Cicero, stung by Bibulus's hostility in every other matter, nonetheless interceded out of old friendship.

Ariobarzanes III Eusebes Philoromaios, king of Cappadocia, whom the Roman Senate had formally recognised through Cicero's mediation; Bibulus's pointed refusal to call him "king" in his dispatches, using instead the periphrasis "son of King Ariobarzanes," was a calculated slight against Cicero.
